([/ʃɛlt, skɛlt/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*SHELT, SKELT*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"); [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *Escaut* [[ɛsko]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:LL-Q150_(fra)-J%C3%A9r%C3%A9my-G%C3%BCnther-Heinz_J%C3%A4hnick-Escaut.wav "File:LL-Q150 (fra)-Jérémy-Günther-Heinz Jähnick-Escaut.wav"); [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"): *Schelde* [[ˈsxɛldə]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Dutch "Help:IPA/Dutch") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Nl-Schelde.ogg "File:Nl-Schelde.ogg")) is a 435-kilometre-long (270 mi)[[4]](#cite_note-4) river that flows through northern [France](/wiki/France "France"), western [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium"), and the southwestern part of [the Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands"), with its mouth at the North Sea. Its name is derived from an adjective corresponding to [Old English](/wiki/Old_English "Old English") *sċeald* ("shallow"), [Modern English](/wiki/Modern_English "Modern English") *shoal*, [Low German](/wiki/Low_German "Low German") *schol*, [West Frisian](/wiki/West_Frisian_language "West Frisian language") *skol*, and obsolete [Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language") *skäll* ("thin").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The **Scheldt** ([/ʃɛlt, skɛlt/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*SHELT, SKELT*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"); [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *Escaut* [[ɛsko]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:LL-Q150_(fra)-J%C3%A9r%C3%A9my-G%C3%BCnther-Heinz_J%C3%A4hnick-Escaut.wav "File:LL-Q150 (fra)-Jérémy-Günther-Heinz Jähnick-Escaut.wav"); [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"): *Schelde* [[ˈsxɛldə]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Dutch "Help:IPA/Dutch") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Nl-Schelde.ogg "File:Nl-Schelde.ogg")) is a 435-kilometre-long (270 mi)[[4]](#cite_note-4) river that flows through northern [France](/wiki/France "France"), western [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium"), and the southwestern part of [the Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands"), with its mouth at the North Sea. Its name is derived from an adjective corresponding to [Old English](/wiki/Old_English "Old English") *sċeald* ("shallow"), [Modern English](/wiki/Modern_English "Modern English") *shoal*, [Low German](/wiki/Low_German "Low German") *schol*, [West Frisian](/wiki/West_Frisian_language "West Frisian language") *skol*, and obsolete [Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language") *skäll* ("thin").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Course

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scheldt&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Course")]

The headwaters of the Scheldt are in [Gouy](/wiki/Gouy,_Aisne "Gouy, Aisne"), in the [Aisne](/wiki/Aisne "Aisne") department of northern France. It flows north through [Cambrai](/wiki/Cambrai "Cambrai") and [Valenciennes](/wiki/Valenciennes "Valenciennes"), and enters Belgium near [Tournai](/wiki/Tournai "Tournai"). [Ghent](/wiki/Ghent "Ghent") developed at the confluence of the [Lys](/wiki/Lys_(river) "Lys (river)"), one of its main tributaries, and the Scheldt, which then turns east. Near [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp"), the largest city on its banks, the Scheldt flows west into the Netherlands toward the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Originally there were two branches from that point: the [Oosterschelde](/wiki/Oosterschelde "Oosterschelde") (Eastern Scheldt); and the [Westerschelde](/wiki/Westerschelde "Westerschelde") (Western Scheldt). In the 19th century, however, the Dutch built a [dyke](/wiki/Levee "Levee") that cuts the river off from its eastern (northern) branch and connects [Zuid-Beveland](/wiki/Zuid-Beveland "Zuid-Beveland") with the mainland ([North Brabant](/wiki/North_Brabant "North Brabant")). Today the river continues into the Westerschelde [estuary](/wiki/Estuary "Estuary") only, passing [Terneuzen](/wiki/Terneuzen "Terneuzen") to reach the North Sea between [Breskens](/wiki/Breskens "Breskens") in [Zeelandic Flanders](/wiki/Zeelandic_Flanders "Zeelandic Flanders") and [Vlissingen](/wiki/Vlissingen "Vlissingen") (Flushing) on [Walcheren](/wiki/Walcheren "Walcheren").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The Scheldt is an important [waterway](/wiki/Waterway "Waterway"), and has been made navigable from its mouth up to Cambrai. Above Cambrai, the [Canal de Saint-Quentin](/wiki/Canal_de_Saint-Quentin "Canal de Saint-Quentin") follows its course. The [port of Antwerp](/wiki/Port_of_Antwerp "Port of Antwerp"), the second-largest in Europe, developed on its banks. Several [canals](/wiki/Canal "Canal") (including the [Scheldt-Rhine Canal](/wiki/Scheldt-Rhine_Canal "Scheldt-Rhine Canal") connecting [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp") to [Rotterdam](/wiki/Rotterdam "Rotterdam"), the [Albert Canal](/wiki/Albert_Canal "Albert Canal") connecting it to [Liège](/wiki/Li%C3%A8ge "Liège") and the [Brussel-Scheldt](/wiki/Brussels%E2%80%93Scheldt_Maritime_Canal "Brussels–Scheldt Maritime Canal"), [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels_Canal "Brussels Canal") and [Brussels-Charleroi](/wiki/Brussels%E2%80%93Charleroi_Canal "Brussels–Charleroi Canal") canals connecting it to [Charleroi](/wiki/Charleroi "Charleroi") via [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels")) connect the Scheldt with the basins of the [Rhine](/wiki/Rhine "Rhine"), [Meuse](/wiki/Meuse_(river) "Meuse (river)"), and [Seine](/wiki/Seine "Seine") rivers, and with the industrial areas around [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels"), [Liège](/wiki/Li%C3%A8ge "Liège"), [Lille](/wiki/Lille "Lille"), [Dunkirk](/wiki/Dunkirk "Dunkirk"), and [Mons](/wiki/Mons,_Belgium "Mons, Belgium").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The Scheldt flows through the following [departments of France](/wiki/Departments_of_France "Departments of France"), [provinces of Belgium](/wiki/Provinces_of_Belgium "Provinces of Belgium"), [provinces of the Netherlands](/wiki/Provinces_of_the_Netherlands "Provinces of the Netherlands"), and towns:[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

* [Aisne](/wiki/Aisne "Aisne") (F): [Gouy](/wiki/Gouy,_Aisne "Gouy, Aisne")
* [Nord](/wiki/Nord_(d%C3%A9partement) "Nord (département)") (F): [Cambrai](/wiki/Cambrai "Cambrai"), [Denain](/wiki/Denain "Denain"), [Valenciennes](/wiki/Valenciennes "Valenciennes")
* [Hainaut](/wiki/Hainaut_(province) "Hainaut (province)") (B): [Tournai](/wiki/Tournai "Tournai")
* [West Flanders](/wiki/West_Flanders "West Flanders") (B): [Avelgem](/wiki/Avelgem "Avelgem")
* [East Flanders](/wiki/East_Flanders "East Flanders") (B): [Oudenaarde](/wiki/Oudenaarde "Oudenaarde"), [Ghent](/wiki/Ghent "Ghent"), [Dendermonde](/wiki/Dendermonde "Dendermonde"), [Temse](/wiki/Temse "Temse")
* [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp_(province) "Antwerp (province)") (B): [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp")
* [Zeeland](/wiki/Zeeland "Zeeland") (NL): [Hulst](/wiki/Hulst "Hulst"), [Terneuzen](/wiki/Terneuzen "Terneuzen"), [Sluis](/wiki/Sluis "Sluis"), [Vlissingen](/wiki/Vlissingen "Vlissingen")

## History

Aerial view of the Scheldt estuary, looking toward Antwerp from the northwest

The Scheldt estuary has always had considerable commercial and strategic importance. Called *Scaldis* in [Roman](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") times, it was important for the shipping lanes to [Roman Britain](/wiki/Roman_Britain "Roman Britain"). [Nehalennia](/wiki/Nehalennia "Nehalennia") was venerated at its mouth. The [Franks](/wiki/Franks "Franks") took control over the region about the year 260 and at first interfered with the Roman supply routes as pirates. Later they became allies of the Romans. With the various divisions of the [Frankish Empire](/wiki/Frankish_Empire "Frankish Empire") in the 9th century, the Scheldt eventually became the border between the Western and Eastern parts of the Empire, which later became France and the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

This status quo remained intact, at least on paper, until 1528, but by then, both the [County of Flanders](/wiki/County_of_Flanders "County of Flanders") on the western bank and Zeeland and the [Duchy of Brabant](/wiki/Duchy_of_Brabant "Duchy of Brabant") on the east were part of the [Habsburg](/wiki/Habsburg "Habsburg") possessions of the [Seventeen Provinces](/wiki/Seventeen_Provinces "Seventeen Provinces"). Antwerp was the most prominent harbour in Western Europe. After this city [fell back under Spanish control](/wiki/Fall_of_Antwerp_(1584-1585) "Fall of Antwerp (1584-1585)") in 1585, the [Dutch Republic](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic") took control of [Zeelandic Flanders](/wiki/Zeelandic_Flanders "Zeelandic Flanders"), a strip of land on the left bank, and closed the Scheldt for shipping. That shifted the trade to the ports of [Amsterdam](/wiki/Amsterdam "Amsterdam") and [Middelburg](/wiki/Middelburg,_Zeeland "Middelburg, Zeeland") and seriously crippled Antwerp, an important and traumatic element in the history of relations between the Netherlands and what was to become Belgium.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Access to the river was the subject of the brief [Kettle War](/wiki/Kettle_War "Kettle War") of 1784, and during the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution") shortly afterwards, the river was reopened in 1792. Once Belgium had claimed its [independence](/wiki/Belgian_revolution "Belgian revolution") from the Netherlands in 1830, the treaty of the Scheldt determined that the river should remain accessible to ships heading for Belgian [ports](/wiki/Port "Port"). Nevertheless, the Dutch government would demand a [toll](/wiki/Fee "Fee") from passing vessels until 16 July 1863.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

*The Question of the Scheldt*, a study providing "a history of the international legal arrangements governing the Western Scheldt", was prepared for the use of British negotiators at the [Treaty of Versailles](/wiki/Treaty_of_Versailles "Treaty of Versailles") in 1919.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

In the [Second World War](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), the Scheldt estuary once again became a contested area. Despite Allied control of [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp"), German forces still occupied fortified positions in September 1944 throughout the Scheldt estuary west and north, preventing any Allied shipping from reaching the port. In the [Battle of the Scheldt](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Scheldt "Battle of the Scheldt"), the [Canadian First Army](/wiki/Canadian_First_Army "Canadian First Army") successfully cleared the area, allowing supply convoys direct access to the port of Antwerp by November 1944.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## Navigation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scheldt&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Navigation")]

[![Location of navigable river Schedt/Escaut](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Escaut_Location.jpg/250px-Escaut_Location.jpg)](/wiki/File:Escaut_Location.jpg)

The navigable river Escaut/Scheldt from Cambrai to the North Sea (from European Waterways Map and Directory, 5th edition)

Canalisation from Cambrai down to Valenciennes was completed in 1788. [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon") saw the benefits of linking Paris to Belgium and accelerated completion of the [Canal de Saint-Quentin](/wiki/Canal_de_Saint-Quentin "Canal de Saint-Quentin") to the south. The locks were deepened and doubled, as coal became the essential commodity of the industrial revolution. Upgrading downstream from Bouchain was started in the 1960s in both France and Flanders, but the waterway is still not fully compliant with European standards. All the locks on the high-capacity section are being doubled by European Class Vb size locks, 185 by 12 m (607 by 39 ft), as part of the overall European Seine-Scheldt waterway project.[[9]](#cite_note-9) The Pont des Trous, a listed fortified bridge in Tournai that has already been substantially modified, will again be raised to provide the necessary dimensions, including an air draught of 7.10 m (23 ft 4 in).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The 13 km section between Cambrai (connection with Canal de Saint-Quentin) and [Hordain](/wiki/Hordain "Hordain") (connection with [Canal de la Sensée](/wiki/Sens%C3%A9e_Canal "Sensée Canal")) is only navigable for small ships ([péniche](/wiki/P%C3%A9niche_(barge) "Péniche (barge)")) and has 5 locks.[[10]](#cite_note-10)
